reading.title: Reading strategy
bullets: Skim first for gist: read the title, headings, and first sentence of each paragraph before tackling questions
Scan for keyword detail: locate the section of the text that matches the question topic before reading closely
For True/False/Not Given: TRUE = the text explicitly agrees, FALSE = the text explicitly contradicts, NOT GIVEN = the information is simply absent — watch for paraphrase
For Matching Headings: identify the main idea of each paragraph (usually in the first or last sentence) and match it to the heading bank
Manage time: roughly 20 minutes per passage; if stuck on one question, move on and return later — do not leave blanks

listening.title: Listening strategy
bullets: Predict answers from the question wording before the audio begins — think about what type of information is needed (name, number, place, reason)
Listen for synonyms and paraphrases, not the exact words in the question
Mind spelling, number formats, and word limits — 'no more than TWO words' means exactly that
If you miss one answer, move on immediately — the audio plays only once and getting behind costs more answers
Use the example answer at the start of Section 1 to tune your ear to the accents and pace

writing.title: Writing strategy
bullets: Task 2 introduction: paraphrase the prompt in your own words, then state your clear position — do not copy the question
Each body paragraph: topic sentence → explanation → specific example → result/implication → linking phrase to next idea
Aim for 270–320 words; fluent, accurate paragraphs beat long, error-filled ones every time
The Band 6→7 jump is depth of explanation and precise vocabulary, not rare or obscure words
Task 1 academic: always include an overview sentence summarising the key trend or most striking feature; report what the data shows, do not give your opinion

speaking.title: Speaking strategy
bullets: Part 2: use the 1-minute prep time to jot keywords (not full sentences) for each bullet on the cue card; speak for the full 2 minutes
Develop every idea — give a reason or example rather than just listing facts; never recite a memorised script
You are assessed on four equal criteria: Fluency and Coherence, Lexical Resource, Grammatical Range and Accuracy, Pronunciation
Natural hesitation fillers ('well', 'let me think') are fine; long silent pauses hurt fluency — keep speaking even if imperfect
Part 3 asks abstract or opinion questions: give a clear position, support it with an example or consequence, then briefly acknowledge a counter-view
